sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 132, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Exit now at bottom-right of gray structure (cols ~31-35), near corridor base. At 130 actions, I need to test if exit can enter the narrow corridor (cols 35-39) at the top. Let me try moving UP from this position.
- Goal model: Move the exit block to overlap with the gap corridor or the player. Testing gap alignment first (fewer actions).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Plan: Move exit to gap area with 2 UP + 2 LEFT to get exit to rows 30-34, cols 30-34 (overlapping gap at rows 30-35, cols 29-33).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 131 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 6.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The exit is now at rows 45-46, cols 49-53. Wait, that's the RIGHT edge of the gray structure. The exit moved UP, but it's still at rows 45-46. It didn't move UP at all!
Wait, the boundary is [[45, 49], [45, 53], [46, 53], [46, 49]]. This means the exit is at rows 45-46, cols 49-53. This is the bottom-right corner of the gray structure.
But I pressed UP, and the exit didn't move UP. It stayed at rows 45-46. So the exit is blocked from moving UP.
Wait, but the exit moved from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53. That's a big jump! The exit moved RIGHT, not UP.
Actually, let me re-read the output. The exit boundary is [[45, 49], [45, 53], [46, 53], [46, 49]]. This means the exit is at rows 45-46, cols 49-53. But I pressed UP, not RIGHT.
Hmm, maybe the exit wrapped around? Or maybe the exit moved to a different position because of some rule I don't understand.
Actually, looking at the image, the exit is at the bottom-right corner of the gray structure. The orange part is visible at the right edge. This is consistent with the boundary [[45, 49], [45, 53], [46, 53], [46, 49]].
But I pressed UP, and the exit moved from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53. That's a jump of 14-18 cols to the RIGHT. This doesn't make sense.
Wait, maybe the exit didn't move at all, and the boundary I printed was from the PREVIOUS frame? No, the code printed the exit boundary from current_frame.segmentation, which should be the current frame.
Let me re-examine. The previous exit position was [[45, 19], [45, 23], [46, 23], [46, 19]] (from the previous turn's output). After pressing RIGHT 12 times, the exit moved to cols 31-35. Then I pressed UP, and the exit is now at cols 49-53.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The exit moved from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53 after pressing UP. That's a jump of 14-18 cols to the RIGHT.
Unless... the exit wrapped around the gray structure? Or maybe the exit teleported to a different position?
Actually, I think I misread the previous output. Let me check again.
Previous turn: "Exit: [[45, 19], [45, 23], [46, 23], [46, 19]]" - this was the exit position BEFORE the RIGHT 12 actions.
After RIGHT 12 actions, the exit should be at cols 31-35 (19+12=31, 23+12=35). But I didn't print the exit position after the RIGHT actions.
Then I pressed UP, and the exit is now at cols 49-53. This suggests the exit moved from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53 after pressing UP. That's a jump of 14-18 cols to the RIGHT.
This is very strange. The exit moved RIGHT when I pressed UP. This suggests the exit is constrained to move along the gray structure's boundary, and pressing UP caused it to move along the boundary in a different direction.
Actually, wait. Maybe the exit is constrained to move along the PERIMETER of the gray structure, and the arrow keys control the direction of movement along the perimeter. In this case, pressing UP might cause the exit to move along the perimeter in the "UP" direction, which could be RIGHT if the exit is at the bottom of the structure.
Let me test this hypothesis. If the exit moves along the perimeter, then:
- Pressing RIGHT moves the exit along the bottom edge (left to right)
- Pressing UP moves the exit along the right edge (bottom to top)
- Pressing LEFT moves the exit along the top edge (right to left)
- Pressing DOWN moves the exit along the left edge (top to bottom)
But this doesn't match my observations. The exit moved RIGHT when I pressed RIGHT, and it moved RIGHT when I pressed UP (from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53).
Hmm, maybe the exit is constrained to the bottom edge of the gray structure, and it can only move LEFT and RIGHT along that edge. In this case, pressing UP or DOWN would have no effect (or cause the exit to move along the bottom edge in some direction).
Actually, looking at the image, the exit is at the bottom-right corner of the gray structure. The orange part is visible at the right edge. The exit seems to be constrained to the bottom edge.
Let me check if the exit can move UP from the right edge. If the exit is at the bottom-right corner, pressing UP might move it along the right edge (upwards).
Let me try pressing UP again to see if the exit moves up the right edge.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Exit at rows 45-46, cols 49-53 (bottom-right corner). Pressing UP caused exit to jump from cols 31-35 to cols 49-53 (moved RIGHT, not UP). Exit seems constrained to bottom edge. Let me try pressing UP again to see if it moves up the right edge.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Exit at rows 45-46, cols 49-53 (bottom-right corner)
# Try pressing UP to see if exit moves up the right edge
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
